message:
malloc_abi: serialize fallback-alloc table access with a spinlock

The `FALLBACK_ALLOC_PTRS` table is a static open-addressed hash-set
of allocation pointers that the fallback allocator has handed out
outside of the main arena. It was being read and written from
multiple threads via a mix of `AtomicUsize` compare/loads on the
slot pointers, but the size-shadow table added for this fix, and
the multi-step "is key resident AND how big is it" reads that
inform unknown-pointer policy decisions, cannot be made consistent
under pure per-slot atomics: a reader can see the pointer
installed but the size still zero, or vice versa.

This commit adds a short, non-blocking, CAS-based spinlock that
serialises every operation that has to observe both the pointer
slot and the size slot together:

- `static FALLBACK_ALLOC_SIZES: [AtomicUsize; FALLBACK_ALLOC_TABLE_SLOTS]`
  — parallel table of allocation sizes indexed by the same open-address
  key as FALLBACK_ALLOC_PTRS.
- `static FALLBACK_ALLOC_TABLE_LOCK: AtomicBool` — the per-table
  spinlock.
- `struct FallbackAllocTableGuard` + `Drop impl` — RAII guard that
  releases the lock with `Ordering::Release` so readers observe a
  consistent (pointer, size) pair published by the writer.
- `fn lock_fallback_alloc_table()` — CAS spin with
  `Ordering::AcqRel` on success, `Acquire` on failure, plus
  `std::hint::spin_loop()` inside the retry arm. No backoff or
  park — the protected sections are O(1) hash probes and the
  spinlock is the right shape here.
- Every call site that touches FALLBACK_ALLOC_PTRS /
  FALLBACK_ALLOC_SIZES now grabs `let _guard =
  lock_fallback_alloc_table();` before the probe loop. The guard is
  dropped at end-of-scope so each function holds the lock for the
  smallest possible region.

Why a spinlock instead of a parking_lot Mutex: this path is
reachable from inside `malloc`/`free` under glibc-compatibility
conditions, which means we cannot allocate while holding the lock
(no heap from inside the allocator) and we cannot block on a
kernel futex (we would deadlock against fork-handlers that also
touch this table). A handcrafted AtomicBool + spin_loop is the
right primitive here, matches what glibc's own arena-lock fallback
looks like, and keeps the cold path off any std::sync::* types
that would pull in extra interior-mutability.

No public ABI or signature change; this is strictly a concurrency
hardening commit. Fuzz runs that were intermittently wedging on
the old "saw a pointer but size==0" read path (leading to
misclassified unknown-pointer free calls under
`fuzz_malloc_fallback`) should now observe deterministic
(pointer, size) pairs.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>
